en.extract:
The black-backed butcherbird (Cracticus mentalis) is a species of bird in the family Artamidae.
It is found in southern New Guinea and Cape York Peninsula in Queensland, Australia.


Taxonomy
The black-backed butcherbird was first described by Italian explorers and naturalists Tommaso Salvadori and  Luigi D'Albertis in 1876. Although its scientific name translates as "mental noisy bird", the species epithet mentalis refers to its chin not its mind. It is one of six (or seven) members of the genus Cracticus known as butcherbirds. Within the genus, it is most closely related to the grey and silver-backed butcherbirds. The three form a monophyletic group—the subgenus Bulestes—within the genus, having diverged from ancestors of the pied butcherbird around five million years ago.
An alternate common name is white-throated butcherbird to distinguish it from the pied butcherbird, which has a black throat.
Gregory Mathews described the Australian population as a separate subspecies kempi in 1912, citing less white plumage on the nape and tail. However Amadon did not observe this difference.
The butcherbirds, Australian magpie and currawongs were placed in the family Cracticidae in 1914 by John Albert Leach after he had studied their musculature. American ornithologists Charles Sibley and Jon Ahlquist recognised the close relationship between woodswallows and the butcherbirds in 1985, and combined them into a Cracticini clade, which became the family Artamidae.


Description
Like other butcherbirds, the black-backed butcherbird is a stockily built bird with a relatively large head and short wings and legs. It is around 25 cm (10 in) long. Australian birds are smaller than those from New Guinea. Its plumage is predominantly black and white. It has a black head and lores, lower back and wings, with white nape, throat and underparts. The wings feathers are edged with white. The rump and base of tail are grey. The eyes are a dark brown, the legs grey and the bill a pale bluish grey tipped with black, with a prominent hook at the end.
The plumage of the juvenile has an untidy brown and white plumage with the same general pattern as the adult, with a darker bill.


Distribution and habitat
The black-backed butcherbird is found in Cape York Peninsula north of the Palmer River, and in the Trans-Fly region and vicinity of Port Moresby in New Guinea up to altitudes of 600 m (2000 ft). The habitat is savannah and open woodland.


Behaviour
The black-backed butcherbird has been observed anting.


Feeding
The black-backed butcherbird is carnivorous, preying on small lizards and birds.


Breeding
Located in the fork of a tree, the nest is constructed of dry sticks with a finer material such as dried grass, bark and leaves forming a cup-shaped interior. The clutch consists of two to four (most commonly three) eggs blotched with brown over a base colour of various shades of pale greyish- or brownish-green, or red. Oval in shape, they are around 27 mm long by 20 mm wide (1.06 in × 0.79 in).


References

The '''black-backed butcherbird''' ('''''Cracticus mentalis''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Artamidae]].
It is found in southern [[New Guinea]] and [[Cape York Peninsula]] in [[Queensland]], Australia.

==Taxonomy==
The black-backed butcherbird was first described by Italian explorers and naturalists [[Tommaso Salvadori]] and  [[Luigi D'Albertis]] in 1876. Although its scientific name translates as "mental noisy bird", the species epithet ''mentalis'' refers to its chin not its mind.<ref name=fraser>{{cite book|last1=Gray|first1=Jeannie|last2=Fraser|first2=Ian|title=Australian Bird Names: A Complete Guide|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=z6ia8rqTa7EC&pg=PA236 |year=2013|publisher=Csiro Publishing|isbn=978-0-643-10471-6|page=236}}</ref> It is one of six (or seven) members of the genus ''[[Cracticus]]'' known as butcherbirds. Within the genus, it is most closely related to the [[grey butcherbird|grey]] and [[silver-backed butcherbird|silver-backed]] butcherbirds. The three form a monophyletic group—the subgenus ''Bulestes''<ref>{{cite book| author=Schodde, Richard| author-link=Richard Schodde|author2=Mason, I.J.|author2-link=Ian J. Mason |year=1999 |title=The Directory of Australian Birds: Passerines. A Taxonomic and Zoogeographic Atlas of the Biodiversity of Birds in Australia and its Territories |location=Collingwood, Australia |publisher= CSIRO Publishing | isbn=978-0-643-10293-4|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XXbJoaGF36YC&q=Black-backed+butcherbird&pg=PT1236}}</ref>—within the genus, having diverged from ancestors of the [[pied butcherbird]] around five million years ago.<ref name="kearns13">{{cite journal|doi=10.1016/j.ympev.2012.11.020|author1=Kearns, Anna |author2=Joseph, Leo |author3=Cook, Lyn G. |year=2013|title=A Multilocus Coalescent Analysis of the Speciational History of the Australo-Papuan Butcherbirds and their Allies|journal=Molecular Phylogenetics and Evolution|volume=66|issue=3|pages=941–52 |pmid=23219707}}</ref>

An alternate common name is white-throated butcherbird to distinguish it from the [[pied butcherbird]], which has a black throat.<ref name=fraser/>

[[Gregory Mathews]] described the Australian population as a separate subspecies ''kempi'' in 1912, citing less white plumage on the nape and tail. However Amadon did not observe this difference.<ref name=Amadon51>{{cite journal|author=Amadon, Dean|year=1951|title=Taxonomic notes on the Australian butcher-birds (family Cracticidae)|journal= American Museum Novitates|issue=1504|pages=1–33|hdl=2246/3960|access-date=20 April 2010|url=http://digitallibrary.amnh.org/dspace/bitstream/handle/2246/3960//v2/dspace/ingest/pdfSource/nov/N1504.pdf?sequence=1}}</ref>

The [[butcherbird]]s, [[Australian magpie]] and [[currawong]]s were placed in the family Cracticidae in 1914 by [[John Albert Leach]] after he had studied their musculature.<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1071/MU914002 |last=Leach |first=John Albert |author-link=John Albert Leach |year=1914|title=The myology of the Bell-Magpie (''Strepera'') and its position in classification |journal=Emu |volume=14 |issue=1 |pages=2–38|url=https://zenodo.org/record/1429902 }}</ref> American ornithologists [[Charles Sibley]] and [[Jon Edward Ahlquist|Jon Ahlquist]] recognised the close relationship between [[woodswallow]]s and the butcherbirds in 1985, and combined them into a Cracticini [[clade]],<ref name="SibAhl85">{{cite journal|doi=10.1071/MU9850001|author1=Sibley, C.G. |author2=Ahlquist, J.E. |year=1985|title=The phylogeny and classification of Australo-Papuan passerine birds|journal=Emu|volume=85|issue=1|pages=1–14|url=http://www.publish.csiro.au/?act=view_file&file_id=MU9850001.pdf|access-date=15 April 2009}}</ref> which became the family Artamidae.<ref name=CB08>{{cite book |title=Systematics and Taxonomy of Australian Birds|url=https://archive.org/details/systematicstaxon00chri|url-access=limited|vauthors=Christidis L, Boles WE |year=2008 |publisher=CSIRO Publishing |location=Canberra |isbn=978-0-643-06511-6 |page=[https://archive.org/details/systematicstaxon00chri/page/n206 196]}}</ref>

==Description==
Like other butcherbirds, the black-backed butcherbird is a stockily built bird with a relatively large head and short wings and legs.<ref name=Amadon51/> It is around 25 cm (10 in) long.<ref name=Slater74/> Australian birds are smaller than those from New Guinea.<ref name=Amadon51/> Its plumage is predominantly black and white. It has a black head and lores, lower back and wings, with white nape, throat and underparts. The wings feathers are edged with white. The rump and base of tail are grey.<ref name=Slater74/> The eyes are a dark brown, the legs grey and the bill a pale bluish grey tipped with black,<ref name=Slater74/> with a prominent hook at the end.<ref name=Amadon51/>

The plumage of the juvenile has an untidy brown and white plumage with the same general pattern as the adult, with a darker bill.<ref name="BNG">{{cite book|author1=Pratt, Thane K. |author2=Beehler, Bruce M. |title=Birds of New Guinea|publisher=Princeton University Press|date=2014|edition=Second|page=440|isbn=978-1-4008-6511-6|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=SBn8AwAAQBAJ&pg=PA440}}</ref>

==Distribution and habitat==
The black-backed butcherbird is found in Cape York Peninsula north of the Palmer River,<ref name=Slater74>{{cite book |title=A Field Guide to Australian Birds: Passerines |last=Slater |first=Peter|year=1974 |publisher=Rigby |location=Adelaide, South Australia |isbn=0-85179-813-6|page=279}}</ref> and in the Trans-Fly region and vicinity of Port Moresby in New Guinea up to altitudes of 600 m (2000 ft).<ref name="BNG"/> The habitat is savannah and open woodland.

==Behaviour==
The black-backed butcherbird has been observed [[Anting (bird activity)|anting]].<ref>{{cite journal|last=Bell|first=H.L.|date=1970|title=Possible anting by the Black-backed Butcherbird|journal=Emu|volume=70|issue=1|page=31|doi=10.1071/MU970031a }}</ref>

===Feeding===
The black-backed butcherbird is carnivorous, preying on small lizards and birds.<ref name="BNG"/>

===Breeding===
Located in the fork of a tree, the nest is constructed of dry sticks with a finer material such as dried grass, bark and leaves forming a cup-shaped interior. The [[Clutch (eggs)|clutch]] consists of two to four (most commonly three) eggs blotched with brown over a base colour of various shades of pale greyish- or brownish-green, or red. Oval in shape, they are around 27 mm long by 20 mm wide ({{convert|27|x|20|mm|disp=output only}}).<ref name=Ber93>{{cite book  | last = Beruldsen  | first = Gordon  | author-link=Gordon Beruldsen|title = Australian Birds: Their Nests and Eggs  | publisher = self  | year = 2003  | location = Kenmore Hills, Queensland  | pages = 372–73 |isbn = 0-646-42798-9}}</ref><!-- cites previous three sentences -->

zh.extract:
黑背钟鹊（台湾作白喉鐘鵲，学名：Cracticus mentalis），是雀形目燕鵙科的一种鸟类。
黑背钟鹊体重约77克，翼长约140.5毫米，嘴峰长约39.2毫米，喙宽度约9.3毫米，喙厚度约12.6毫米，跗蹠长约29.3毫米，尾长约99毫米。黑背钟鹊在其分布区内为留鸟，其栖息在半开放的中等高度的林地中，食性为肉食性，主要食物来源是陆生无脊椎动物。


亚种
C. m. mentalis：分布于新几内亚东南部的稀树草原。
C. m. kempi：分布于昆士兰北部约克角半岛。
